name: Jewel Tide Ebbing
description: The Jewel of Ebbing Tide, also known as the Tide-Retreating Jewel (干珠, Hihiruhi no Tama), is one of two magical jewels possessed by Ryūjin, the Dragon God of the Sea in Japanese mythology. The counterpart to this jewel is the Jewel of Flowing Tide (満珠, Michihiruhi no Tama), which controls the rising tide. The Jewel of Ebbing Tide grants the power to control the ebb and flow of the ocean, allowing its wielder to drain vast bodies of water, expose seabeds, and manipulate currents with immense force. According to legend, Empress Jingū used both jewels during her conquest of Korea, employing the Jewel of Ebbing Tide to force the Korean fleet aground and the Jewel of Flowing Tide to drown enemy soldiers. The jewels are symbols of Ryūjin's dominion over the seas and represent the cyclical nature of the tides, embodying both destruction and creation. Possession of the Jewel of Ebbing Tide grants immense power, capable of altering landscapes and controlling naval warfare. However, misuse of its power could lead to catastrophic droughts, ecological disasters, and the wrath of Ryūjin himself. The jewel is often depicted as a shimmering orb, pulsing with an inner light that reflects the vastness and power of the ocean. Its surface is said to be cool to the touch, yet radiating an ancient and formidable energy. Legends say the jewel is imbued with Ryūjin's own essence, making it a sacred artifact of immense importance.
subtitle: Controls the Ebbing of Tides
origin: Created by Ryūjin, the Dragon God of the Sea.
currentLocation: Said to be hidden within Ryūgū-jō, the Dragon Palace beneath the sea.

keyMyths.title: Empress Jingū's Conquest
keyMyths.description: Empress Jingū used the Jewel of Ebbing Tide to lower the sea level, grounding the Korean fleet and securing victory. This act demonstrated the jewel's immense power in naval warfare.

keyMyths.title: Ryūjin's Power
keyMyths.description: The jewels represent Ryūjin's control over the tides, essential for maintaining balance in the ocean ecosystem. The power to ebb and flow is central to his dominion.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation and annotation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation and annotation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.description: Translation and annotation of the Nihongi (Nihon Shoki), another important source of Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation and annotation of the Nihongi (Nihon Shoki), another important source of Japanese mythology.

worshipPractices.name: Sea Offerings
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, offerings of sake, rice, and other valuable items are sometimes made to the sea to appease Ryūjin and ensure the continued ebb and flow of the tides, indirectly acknowledging the power of the Jewel of Ebbing Tide. These offerings are often performed by fishermen or coastal communities dependent on the sea's bounty.

festivals.name: Sea Purification Rituals
festivals.timing: Varies regionally, often coinciding with changing tides or seasons.
festivals.description: During these rituals, communities perform ceremonies to cleanse the sea and pray for continued prosperity and safety. These ceremonies can involve cleansing rituals with salt and water, symbolizing the ebb and flow of the tides and the removal of misfortune.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of controlling the tides resonates in modern Japanese culture, often appearing in fantasy literature, anime, and video games where characters possess similar abilities to manipulate water. The image of a powerful jewel capable of manipulating the sea's ebb and flow remains a potent symbol of natural power and control.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like the Jewel of Ebbing Tide controls the sea, the Iron Gloves enable Thor to control Mjolnir's power, preventing its destructive force. Both are tools for managing immense, potentially chaotic energy.

Poseidon's Trident grants him dominion over the seas and the ability to control tides and storms, mirroring the Jewel's power to ebb the tide. Both are symbols of authority over the marine environment.

cross_cultural_parallels.entity: Anuket's control over the Nile's flood
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Anuket was believed to control the flooding of the Nile, essential for agriculture. The Jewel of Ebbing Tide similarly controls the sea's ebb, creating balance and allowing access to previously submerged areas.

Manannán mac Lir's cloak (or the mist he conjured) could conceal or reveal islands, creating or removing access to them, much like the Jewel of Ebbing Tide reveals land by retracting the water. Both control visibility and accessibility related to the sea.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: The ebbing tide is fundamentally linked to the element of water, representing its withdrawal and retreat.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The tides are often associated with the lunar cycle, signifying a cyclical ebb and flow controlled by celestial forces.

associations.name: Deep Blue
associations.significance: Represents the vastness of the ocean from which the tide is receding, hinting at unseen depths.

associations.name: Decline
associations.significance: The ebbing tide symbolizes decline and the diminishing of power or influence as the water recedes.

associations.name: Two
associations.significance: Reflects the duality of the Jewel of Ebbing Tide and the Jewel of Flowing Tide, representing opposing forces.

associations.name: Crab
associations.significance: Crabs are creatures that emerge from the seabed as the tide ebbs, representing vulnerability exposed.

associations.name: Vulnerability
associations.significance: The ebbing tide reveals the seabed, exposing vulnerable creatures and highlighting a period of susceptibility.

companions.name: Ryūjin
companions.description: Ryūjin is the Dragon God of the Sea and the owner of the Jewel of Ebbing Tide. He entrusts the jewel to those he deems worthy or uses it himself to control the tides.

companions.name: Watatsumi
companions.description: Watatsumi is another powerful sea deity often associated with Ryūjin, and potentially a previous owner or guardian of the jewel. He is a benevolent protector of the ocean's inhabitants.

companions.name: Urashima Tarō
companions.description: Urashima Tarō, a fisherman, is sometimes granted access to Ryūjin's palace and might be entrusted with the jewel for a specific purpose. He could be a temporary wielder of the jewel's power.

companions.name: Octopus (Tako)
companions.description: Octopus often serve Ryūjin in his underwater palace. They could be tasked with guarding the jewel or assisting in its use.

companions.name: Kame (Sea Turtle)
companions.description: Sea Turtles are loyal servants of Ryūjin, known for their wisdom and longevity. They could be guardians or messengers associated with the Jewel of Ebbing Tide.

extendedContent.title: Creation & Forging by the Dragon God
extendedContent.content:
The precise origins of the Jewel of Ebbing Tide are shrouded in the mists of Japanese creation myths, but its existence is inextricably linked to Ryūjin, the Dragon God of the Sea. While no single origin story explicitly details its forging, it's understood that the jewel's creation is concurrent with, or perhaps even predates, the formation of the Japanese archipelago itself. Ryūjin, as the master of the seas and its myriad treasures, is believed to have either crafted the jewel himself through his divine power or discovered it within the deepest, most inaccessible parts of his underwater kingdom, a realm brimming with magical artifacts and potent energies. The jewel isn't merely a beautiful object; it's a concentrated embodiment of Ryūjin's authority over the tides, a tangible manifestation of his control over the very rhythms of the ocean. 

Considering Ryūjin's role as a guardian and regulator of the marine world, the crafting or acquisition of the Jewel of Ebbing Tide likely involved a ritualistic or transformative process. It’s reasonable to assume that potent natural elements, perhaps imbued with the spiritual essence of the sea itself, were utilized. The 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' (Chronicles of Japan), while not detailing the forging directly, frequently reference Ryūjin's immense power and his ability to command storms, summon sea creatures, and manipulate the tides. These narratives implicitly support the notion that the jewel's creation was a feat of divine craftsmanship, reflecting Ryūjin's mastery over the natural world. 

The creation of the jewel, therefore, represents not only Ryūjin’s dominion but also the delicate balance he maintains between the land and the sea, the ebb and flow of life itself. The jewel becomes a symbol of this cosmic harmony, a testament to the Dragon God’s responsibility in ensuring the stability of the world order.

extendedContent.title: Powers & Properties: Controlling the Retreating Tide
extendedContent.content:
The primary power of the Jewel of Ebbing Tide, or 'Hihiruhi no Tama,' lies in its ability to manipulate the ebb, or receding, of the ocean's tides. This control is absolute, allowing the wielder to command the sea to withdraw at will, exposing seabeds, creating pathways, and potentially stranding ships. The jewel's magic isn't merely a passive manipulation of water levels; it's an active exertion of force, pulling the ocean back as if commanded by a powerful hand. This ability carries immense strategic and practical implications, capable of altering landscapes, facilitating travel, and even influencing battles fought near the coast.

Beyond simply causing the tide to recede, the Jewel of Ebbing Tide likely possesses further, more nuanced properties related to water manipulation. It might allow for the creation of whirlpools, the control of underwater currents, or even the purification of water sources. The extent of these secondary powers remains largely unexplored in the primary sources, but the jewel's inherent connection to Ryūjin, a deity associated with all aspects of the sea, suggests a broader range of capabilities. Imagine the control over the ocean's depths, manipulating currents to confound enemies or even creating temporary islands by solidifying underwater sediment. 

The Jewel of Ebbing Tide’s power is not without limitations. It is implied that the use of the jewel necessitates a connection to the sea, either through lineage (as with the descendants of Ryūjin) or through a deep understanding of its rhythms. Furthermore, the continuous manipulation of the tides could potentially disrupt the natural balance, leading to ecological consequences or incurring the wrath of Ryūjin himself. This highlights the importance of responsible use and a respect for the power the jewel represents.

extendedContent.title: Notable Wielders: Descendants and Borrowers
extendedContent.content:
While Ryūjin is the ultimate owner and source of the Jewel of Ebbing Tide's power, several figures in Japanese mythology are known to have wielded or interacted with it, primarily through Ryūjin's direct intervention or through familial ties. The most prominent example is Hoderi (Hoori), also known as Yamasachi-hiko, grandson of Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu. In the tale of the lost fishhook, Hoderi journeys to Ryūjin's underwater palace and eventually borrows both the Jewel of Flowing Tide and the Jewel of Ebbing Tide to punish his elder brother, Hoori (Umisachi-hiko), who had refused to return the lost hook. Hoderi uses the Jewel of Ebbing Tide to torment his brother by receding the tide and causing him great distress. 

Although Hoderi is the most well-known wielder, the narrative emphasizes that he is merely *borrowing* the jewel under Ryūjin's authority. This highlights the Dragon God's ultimate control over the jewels and their power. The tale also suggests that descendants of Ryūjin, even if not divine themselves, possess a certain affinity for the jewels and their power, making them potential candidates for wielding them, albeit with the Dragon God's permission. The success of Hoderi in utilizing both jewels shows that even a mortal, with the blessing of Ryūjin, can command the power of the tides. 

It's important to note that the stories don't portray anyone other than those directly associated with Ryūjin as wielding the jewel. This reinforces the notion that the jewels are not simply magical objects to be found and used by anyone. They are intrinsically linked to the Dragon God and his lineage, suggesting a certain level of responsibility and respect is required to wield their power safely and effectively.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism: Control, Balance, and Divine Authority
extendedContent.content:
The Jewel of Ebbing Tide, beyond its practical power to control the seas, carries profound symbolic weight within Japanese mythology. It represents control over natural forces, specifically the ebb and flow of the tides, which in turn symbolizes the cyclical nature of life, death, and renewal. The receding tide can be seen as a metaphor for decline, withdrawal, or even death, but also as a necessary precursor to the return of the tide, representing rebirth and regeneration. The jewel, therefore, embodies the acceptance and understanding of these natural cycles.

Furthermore, the Jewel of Ebbing Tide symbolizes the delicate balance between opposing forces. It exists in tandem with the Jewel of Flowing Tide, highlighting the need for equilibrium between expansion and contraction, activity and rest. The two jewels, when used in conjunction, represent a harmonious control over the marine environment, demonstrating the importance of maintaining balance in all aspects of life. This concept aligns with broader Shinto beliefs emphasizing harmony with nature and the avoidance of extremes.

Finally, the jewel is a symbol of Ryūjin's divine authority and his role as a guardian of the seas. It represents his power to command the elements and his responsibility to maintain order within his domain. The fact that the jewel is not easily accessible to mortals reinforces Ryūjin's status as a powerful and respected deity. The Jewel of Ebbing Tide, therefore, serves as a reminder of the power and influence of the divine realm and the importance of respecting the natural forces that govern the world.

shortDescription: A magical jewel from Ryūjin, the Dragon God, that controls the ebb tide. It counters the Jewel of Flowing Tide, which controls the rising tide.

wielders.name: Ryūjin
wielders.context: Original owner and creator of the jewel.

wielders.name: Empress Jingū
wielders.context: Borrowed the jewel to aid in her conquest of Korea.
